issue_title: Extend `HardCodedNodesPeerPool` class to also do discovery.
issue_body:
### What is wrong?

We currently use a stop-gap solution for working around v4 discovery, `p2p.peer.HardCodedNodesPeerPool`.  It contains known nodes to connect to, specifically for LES connections.

This approach is problematic because these nodes are not guaranteed to be online or to have capacity so.  If they are, then Trinity will not be able to connect until a slot becomes available.  Worse, there's no way without code modification to add new nodes to this list.

### How can it be fixed

Extend how we use this class to to *also* do discovery.  For the first iteration it should treat the hard coded list the same as any nodes that have been connected to via discovery.

Also, add a new CLI flag which can be used to add nodes to this list.

```bash
$ trinity --static-node "enode://deadbeef...@1.2.3.4:30303"
```

This flag should be accepted multiple times to allow specifying multiple nodes.

>    web3.py uses pbkdf2(dklen:32, c:1000000, prf: hmac-sha256).
    these are pretty low defaults
    should def at least use scrypt as a baseline
    hmac-sha256 is much easier to bruteforce, by like 3-4 orders of magnitude
    the parameters don't matter i think the KDF func/lib should be able to accommodate but probably best to make them match the geth defaults
    which are more secure. for some comparison an iteration of the default SCRYPT parameters in geth takes 200-400ms but an iteration of the HMAC KDF takes less than 1ms, on GPUs SCRYPT with r=8 destroys performance because of the high memory bandwidth > requirement, so even on super high end GPUs you can barely get 20-30h/s where as with the HMAC KDF you can get 100k-1m h/s a second.

issue_title: Allow extra package information in repodata.json
issue_body:
With binstar allowing more people to upload packages and channels to specific platforms, it would be great to extend the repodata.json format to include package error messages for example:

``` json
packages: [ ... 
    "matplotlib": {
       "error": "not_available",   
       "message": "Package not available on this platform, this package exits on ['linux-32']",
       ""
    },
...
```

Then when a user runs conda, they can get a better error message:

```
conda install -c binstar --override-channels matplotlib
Error: Package not available on this platform, this package exits on ['linux-32']
```

Instead of erasing existing contents of the profile file, the installer should append its modifications.

issue_title: conda environments should set PIP_CONFIG_FILE to /dev/null
issue_body:
My `~/.config/pip/pip.conf` contains the lines

```
[install]
user = true
```

so that when working with the system python, `pip install foo` does a `--user` install (I never want to do a system-wide install, my linux package manager is there for that).  (See https://pip.pypa.io/en/stable/user_guide/#config-file.)

But when in a conda env, this doesn't make much sense -- you just want to install into the environment.  Two ways to do this would be either to set `PIP_CONFIG_FILE` to `/dev/null` or possibly provide a `pip.conf` which undoes such changes in the environment itself as the environment config file is read last (this seems harder to do -- you'd probably want to undo all possible customizations, which seems awkward).

                          https://conda.anaconda.org/intel/noarch/  (offline)
                          https://repo.continuum.io/pkgs/free/linux-64/  (offline)
                          https://repo.continuum.io/pkgs/free/noarch/  (offline)
                          https://repo.continuum.io/pkgs/pro/linux-64/  (offline)
                          https://repo.continuum.io/pkgs/pro/noarch/  (offline)
            config file : /nfs/site/home/tmtomash/.condarc
           offline mode : True
```

As can be seen, the user-level condarc settings for "offline" and "channels" are both in effect, and the root environment condarc is being ignored.

SECOND issue:
Perhaps this is a feature, but it is unexpected. When specifying a configuration file by using the environment variable CONDARC, conda appears to merge the settings in the listed config file with those in the user's config file, rather than using only the contents of the specified file.

Config file at /localdisk/work/tmtomash/miniconda3/envs/ipytest2/.condarc
`offline: False`

User config file is same as issue #1 above.
```
Current conda install:
               platform : linux-64
          conda version : 4.2.6
       conda is private : False
      conda-env version : 4.2.6
    conda-build version : 2.0.1
         python version : 3.5.2.final.0
       requests version : 2.9.1
       root environment : /localdisk/work/tmtomash/miniconda3  (writable)
    default environment : /localdisk/work/tmtomash/miniconda3
       envs directories : /localdisk/work/tmtomash/miniconda3/envs
          package cache : /localdisk/work/tmtomash/miniconda3/pkgs
           channel URLs : https://conda.anaconda.org/intel/linux-64/
                          https://conda.anaconda.org/intel/noarch/
                          https://repo.continuum.io/pkgs/free/linux-64/
                          https://repo.continuum.io/pkgs/free/noarch/
                          https://repo.continuum.io/pkgs/pro/linux-64/
                          https://repo.continuum.io/pkgs/pro/noarch/
            config file : /localdisk/work/tmtomash/miniconda3/envs/ipytest2/.condarc
           offline mode : False
```
As you can see, the config file is listed as the file specified in CONDARC, and offline=False as that config file states, but the "intel" channel is still being pulled from the user configuration file.

Ultimately, I would like the ability to have environment-specific condarc files so that someone can create a single environment with the "intel" channel without that meaning ALL their environments now prefer intel packages. One potential implementation was to set CONDARC=<env_root>/.condarc, perhaps when "activate"ing the environment. I'm not certain I can rely on any of the current config resolution mechanics, though.

issue_title: Can't build from a symbolic link -- UNHELPFUL error message
issue_body:
_From @remram44 on July 19, 2018 21:41_

### Actual Behavior

```
conda.CondaError: Downloaded bytes did not match Content-Length
  url: file:///tmp/rr_conda_Wg2K6Htj/usagestats.tar.gz
  target_path: /tmp/rr_conda_Wg2K6Htj/croot/src_cache/usagestats.tar.gz
  Content-Length: 44
  downloaded bytes: 14374
```
when `/tmp/rr_conda_Wg2K6Htj/usagestats.tar.gz` is a symbolic link

### Expected Behavior

`file://` URLs to symbolic links in recipes should not break with this unhelpful error (no Content-Length is involved! This is not even HTTP!)

### Other info

This is probably what I was trying to troubleshoot in https://github.com/conda/conda/issues/4134#issuecomment-314097550. The reported size doesn't match the compressed size or the decompressed size, because it is the length of the path in bytes.

You should probably change an `lstat()` to a `stat()` somewhere in your size-checking logic.

### Steps to Reproduce

Point a recipe's `source.url` to a `file://` URL that is a symbolic link.

issue_title: conda fails if a user's home directory doesn't exist
issue_body:
In certain applications that we support, it is possible that a user does not have a home directory within which to create `~/.conda`. This is causing a fatal error in the `append_env`  method [here](https://github.com/conda/conda/blob/master/conda/misc.py#L185-L193).

Note that this function is already designed to fail silently if there is an `IOError`. However, if the home directory itself does not exist, the `os.mkdir` command fails with an `OSError` instead. I would suggest that this exception type be included in the `except` line as well.

issue_title: Not possible to open 2+ config files into same configobj instance?
issue_body:
It does not seem possible to load 2 or more files into the same configobj instance. 

In configparser, You could load multiple files into the same dictionary because it had a separate file load function, like so:

sysconf = configparser.SafeConfigParser()
sysconf.read(file1)
sysconf.read(file2)

Because configobj loads the file in it's class init, this does not seem possible for configobj in it's current state

loading multiple files is useful for when you have a large set of configuration options that have to be human readable and may need to be partially duplicated with slightly different settings for some sections 

The human readable part is why I don't move to xml, and why the nesting limitation in configparser gives me a headache.

(Granted, I just found cofigobj when searching for a solution to human readable arbitrary nesting alternatives for configparser and only skimmed the documentation, so I may have missed something that wasn't obvious.)

@henrikstranneheim wants to put gene description into the variant detail page. I can't find it in the ODM - what's the status?

> Add the vcf annotation “Gene_description” to the variant page where appropriate in layout.

issue_title: Problems with uploading a new gene panel
issue_body:
- [x] The error message is cryptic `Line 1 is malformed` when using the interface.
- [x] CLI throws an error when `version` not supplied

While working on #19 I've uncovered a few test failures which should probably be addressed.

For example, on 32-bit systems, [`test_basic`](https://github.com/HdrHistogram/HdrHistogram_py/blob/17114a6783c121bc84b12812bbb39351195e7b36/test/test_hdrhistogram.py#L58) fails with `AssertionError: 21 != 22`. This is due to [`get_bucket_count()`](https://github.com/HdrHistogram/HdrHistogram_py/blob/17114a6783c121bc84b12812bbb39351195e7b36/hdrh/histogram.py#L34) using `sys.maxsize` to calculate bucket count, which makes it return `22` on 64-bit platforms and `21` on 32-bit based ones. Not sure yet if it's calculation bug or `sys.maxsize` should be replaced with something else in the calculation (can you clarify?).

On the same 32-bit system `test_hdr_payload` just crashes pytest. No idea about how this happens yet.

I also get the impression that the project would benefit from having a CI set up: Appveyor for regularly running Windows tests, TravisCI for doing the same on Linux/Mac. I can provide some help with setting this up, if you're interested.

issue_title: Question/Doc enhancement: Bringing VUnit runner functionality to existing testbenches
issue_body:
How to use VUnit for completely new testbenches is pretty well documented, starting with the most basic cases shown in the user guide.

I think a common questions for someone new to VUnit is: _What do I need to do to adapt existing/legacy testbenches to utilize VUnit?_

Developing an automated testbench is good practice, so it is safe to assume that users out there already have test code that was written without VUnit in mind. Without VUnit, a common practice would probably be to lump a variety of tests into a single testbench for a single entity. The testbench is then run and failures are reported via assertions, and the testbench is automatically stopped if the severity is high enough. If there are no failures, the testbench would end automatically (after a fixed duration, or with a call to `std.env.finish`).

Assuming someone has a set of existing testbenches that are written in such a manner, what is the path of least resistance to adopting the VUnit python running framework (automatic compilation, test detection, parallel simulation, reporting results via XML)?  Obviously some of the benefits of the VUnit VHDL test library will be missed, but for legacy code this is acceptable -- the testbench is already complete.

issue_title: update option "doc_as_upsert" needs text string "true" instead of boolean "True"
issue_body:
Hi

Tried using "doc_as_upsert" in my updates. If the body of my request had a boolean value (ie "'doc_as_upsert': True") it didn't create new docs (it updated the existing ones, though). I needed to use the a text string (ie "'doc_as_upsert' : 'true'"), and then it created new documents. 

That means this call doesn't create new documents:
```
es.update(index="my_index", doc_type="my_doc_type", id=my_id, body={"doc":my_dict_update,'doc_as_upsert':True})```

But this call does:
```
es.update(index="my_index", doc_type="my_doc_type", id=my_id, body={"doc":my_dict_update,'doc_as_upsert':'true'})```

Maybe it's not a bug and it should work like that, as elasticsearch is expecting the string and not the boolean. In that case, it would be a good idea to add a note to the documentation.

Thanks again!

issue_title: SSL upload, bind, unbind commands broken when a web app and its service plan are in different resource groups
issue_body:
### Environment summary

**Install Method:** How did you install the CLI? (e.g. pip, interactive script, apt-get, Docker, MSI, nightly)  
Interactive
**CLI Version:** What version of the CLI and modules are installed? (Available with `az --version`)  
2.0.2
**OS Version:** What OS and version are you using?  
Mac OSX 10.11.6
**Shell Type:** What shell are you using? (e.g. bash, cmd.exe, PowerShell)  
Bash
### Description
When an app service plan is in resource group A and the web app is in resource group B, the `az appservice web config ssl bind|unbind|upload` commands will not work as expected. You'll be able to `upload`, but not `bind` or `unbind` any certs. 

Judging from how the Azure Portal behaves, the commands should be using the resource group of the app service plan rather than the web app for storing/retrieving the cert, e.g. https://github.com/Azure/azure-cli/blob/2793bcddf8876661227d3fb3efd34f105b79f8a3/src/command_modules/azure-cli-appservice/azure/cli/command_modules/appservice/custom.py#L851 should look more like
```
def upload_ssl_cert(resource_group_name, name, certificate_password, certificate_file):
    client = web_client_factory()
    webapp = _generic_site_operation(resource_group_name, name, 'get')
    cert_resource_group_name = _get_resource_group_name_by_resource_id(webapp.server_farm_id)
    cert_file = open(certificate_file, 'rb')
    cert_contents = cert_file.read()
    hosting_environment_profile_param = webapp.hosting_environment_profile
    if hosting_environment_profile_param is None:
        hosting_environment_profile_param = ""

    thumb_print = _get_cert(certificate_password, certificate_file)
    cert_name = _generate_cert_name(thumb_print, hosting_environment_profile_param,
                                    webapp.location, cert_resource_group_name)
    cert = Certificate(password=certificate_password, pfx_blob=cert_contents,
                       location=webapp.location)
    return client.certificates.create_or_update(cert_resource_group_name, cert_name, cert)

def _get_resource_group_name_by_resource_id(resource_id):
    '''Returns the resource group name from parsing the resource id.
    :param str resource_id: The resource id
    '''
    resource_id = resource_id.lower()
    resource_group_keyword = '/resourcegroups/'
    return resource_id[resource_id.index(resource_group_keyword) + len(resource_group_keyword):
                       resource_id.index('/providers/')]
```

issue_title: [DNS] Removing a record from a record set should have a confirmation prompt
issue_body:
If I remove a record set, I get a confirmation prompt (which I can suppress with '--yes')

Removing a record from a record set is just as potentially dangerous. Most record sets contain a single record, and by default removing the last record also removes the record set--so it's probably exactly the same, in fact. However, removing a record from a record set does not include a confirmation prompt.

For consistency, and also to alert users to a potentially dangerous change, we should support a confirmation prompt (suppressable via '--yes') on 'az network dns record-set remove-record', for all record types.

issue_title: [ACR] az acr build times out quicker than expected in some clients
issue_body:
**Describe the bug**

`az acr build` does a server-side and client-side check to determine when the client should stop polling for logs. IE: if UTC server write time - client's current time >= 30 min and there's no logs, stop polling. Sometimes clients' clocks aren't synchronized and this can cause the client to immediately stop polling for additional logs, which is an unfriendly user experience. The fix for this would be to make it purely server or client side based rather than mixing and matching them. Another approach would be to make it strictly logical rather than physical time.

issue_body:
Issues with 'az network dns zone update':
1. -h help text has two groups of parameters both under the same heading 'Generic Update Arguments'.  These should be grouped into a single group, containing add/remove/set
2. Not clear how add/remove/set should be used.  For example, how do I add, edit or remove ARM Tags using this command?  Needs concrete examples.
3. Supporting If-none-match is pointless, it's only useful when creating zones, and that should be done via 'az network dns zone create'.  We should support the option to control create-if-not-exist there, not here.

issue_title: Generic Update needs to support custom setter and getter
issue_body: Most generic updates use the SDK for the getter and setter and a custom method for the convenience arguments, so the Knack generic update was set up like that. However, there are situations where the getter and/or setter come from a custom source, so it needs to support that.

issue_title: pool list -o json cannot be used for pool create --json-file
issue_body:
Dumping an existing pool into json format using `pool list -o json`, cannot later be imported into `pool create --json-file`. The reason being, the serialized json contains `nodeAgentSkuId` while the correct letter-case is `nodeAgentSKUId` (KU capitalized now). 

As you can imagine, some hair pulling was involved :)

Perhaps a unit test for that would be useful

Examples:

* http://data.openaddresses.io/runs/1429251191.158/us-pa-beaver.txt
* http://data.openaddresses.io/runs/1429251191.158/us-ia-polk.txt
* http://data.openaddresses.io/runs/1429251191.158/us-la-lafayette.txt

Result zips can be compressed significantly better if simlar rows are nearby.

This was experienced at https://github.com/openaddresses/openaddresses/pull/3168 , where ordering the csv rows by housenubmer,street,....postcode,id reduced the end zip file size in half.

I propose to order the result by all columns between coordinates and hash, so by: housenubmer, street, unit, city, district, region, postcode, id

If possible also consider moving the unit out of the otherwise most simillar parts of the row (to before housenubmer or after zip).

issue_title: Add filename pattern to *trigger* run on all files
issue_body:
For sake of example, consider a hook that checks C function signature against header files in some way.

Such a hook would run only on files matching `\.c$` - but ideally would be _triggered_ by those matching `\.(c|h)$`.

It would be awesome if in the hooks.yaml and .pre-commit-config.yaml files a hook can be configured similarly to:
```yaml
files: \.c$
always_run: \.h$ 
```

I put it on `always_run` since I think that fits quite neatly - `test_all_when` or something might be a suitable alias:
- when `true`, the hook would run on all (not just staged) matching files, as it does currently;
- when a pattern, the hook would run on all (not just staged) matching files providing a staged file matches the `always_run` pattern

issue_title: TCPSite name property returns incorrect URL when ephemeral ports are used
issue_body:
## Long story short

The ``aiohttp.web.TCPSite`` object provides a ``name`` property allowing a developer to access the URL provided by the site. When using ephemeral ports the URL returned is wrong.

### Long story

After upgrading to aiohttp 3.2.1 I observed the following deprecation warnings:
```
DeprecationWarning: Application.make_handler(...) is deprecated, use AppRunner API instead
```
I investigated AppRunner API and modified my code accordingly to upgrade it. I then observed errors in unit tests that were related to the requesting aiohttp to use ephemeral ports for service endpoints.

In my Python async applications one or more aiohttp servers are running within an application to provide services such as application metrics, diagnostics, etc. Many applications run on a node (no containers here). Ephemeral ports are used to avoid port conflicts between application services and a service discovery mechanism is used to help resolve ephemeral services. I also use ephemeral ports in unit tests to avoid port conflicts with system service ports and other CI jobs running in parallel.

After starting aiohttp web services within my application I fetch their actual URL and register it with a service discovery mechanism. The URL returned from aiohttp is wrong.

## Expected behaviour

Accessing the ``name`` property of a started ``TCPSite`` object returns the correct URL string.

## Actual behaviour

Accessing the ``name`` property of a started ``TCPSite`` object returns the incorrect port in the URL string when ephemeral ports are used.

## Steps to reproduce

Starting a TCPSite with an ephemeral port (port=0) can be used to reproduce the problem. The following script demonstrates this.
``` python
import asyncio
import aiohttp
import aiohttp.web
from yarl import URL


async def demo():
    addr = "127.0.0.1"
    port = 0
    ssl = None

    # Start web site.
    myapp = aiohttp.web.Application()
    runner = aiohttp.web.AppRunner(myapp)
    await runner.setup()

    site = aiohttp.web.TCPSite(runner, addr, port, ssl_context=ssl)
    await site.start()

    # Service is now operational - fetch url to it can be registered with service discovery.

    # fetch URL using aiohttp API call
    site_url_api = site.name

    # fetch URL using my work-around that gets the actual port in use.
    # NOTE: getsockname => IPv4 returns 2-tuple, IPv6 returns 4-tuple
    actual_host, actual_port, *_ = site._server.sockets[0].getsockname()
    site_url_workaround = str(
        URL.build(
            scheme='https' if ssl is not None else 'http',
            host=actual_host if ":" not in actual_host else "[{}]",  # handle IPv4 and IPv6
            port=actual_port))

    # Check if aiohttp returns the correct URL
    if site_url_api != site_url_workaround:
        print(f'ERROR: aiohttp url: {site_url_api} != actual url: {site_url_workaround}')

    # Stop web site.
    await site.stop()
    await runner.cleanup()
    loop = asyncio.get_event_loop()
    await loop.shutdown_asyncgens()


if __name__ == '__main__':
    loop = asyncio.get_event_loop()
    loop.run_until_complete(demo())

```

When I run the script I observe:
```
ERROR: aiohttp url: http://127.0.0.1 != actual url: http://127.0.0.1:54804
```
## Your environment

aiohttp==3.2.1
Mac OSX
'server'

Long text, please read carefully.

My initial idea was supporting both *url mapper* (implemented as `UrlDispatcher` class) and *traversal*.
It follows Pyramid routing schema in some way.
Traversal approach should be done by alternative router implementation.

In fact the idea has failed: too many aiohttp based libraries assumes that router is *url mapper*. Moreover `app.add_subapp` explicitly ignores the fact of alternative implementation existence.

Let's state the fact: the router is `UrlDispatcher`. I want to go even further: deprecate *router* argument in `web.Application`. If user need a custom *url mapper based* router usually it means he adds a couple helper methods for registering resources **on top** of existing `.add_resource()`/`.add_route()`. Better to solve the need by wrapping the whole application by user-specific class with appropriate methods.

In practice it means raising up abstract classes from `web_urldispatcher.py` to `abc.py`.

Also I would add `add_static` and `add_routes` shortcuts to `web.Application`. People should use either URL table or `RouteTableDef` for defining routes, all others are internals. And we have `app.add_subapp` already, let's keep all casual route table definition in the same place. Other `add_*` methods are considered as low-level API and will not be replicated in application.

P.S.
*traversal* still could be built on top of *url mapper*: just make a resource for matching *all* paths like `'/{tail:.+}'` and do what you want in web handler.

" class aiohttp.ClientSession(*, connector=None, loop=None, cookies=None, headers=None, skip_auto_headers=None, auth=None, json_serialize=json.dumps, version=aiohttp.HttpVersion11, cookie_jar=None, read_timeout=None, conn_timeout=None, timeout=sentinel, raise_for_status=False, connector_owner=True, auto_decompress=True, proxies=None)

timeout – a ClientTimeout settings structure, 5min
total timeout by default. "
This is from https://docs.aiohttp.org/en/stable/client_reference.html. But I don't understand if it means that the timeout for each get/post request or the timeout for the connection pool. And if it is for the connection pool, how can I set the timeout for each request? Like, http://docs.python-requests.org/en/master/user/quickstart/#timeouts.

issue_title: Possible security vulnerability to connect to notebook from another tab in the browser or another browser and execute arbitrary code
issue_body:
I saw that Guile just fixed a method that a browser could connect to it even if it only listened on localhost and possibly run arbitrary code (https://lists.gnu.org/archive/html/guile-user/2016-10/msg00007.html). It seems like Jupyter notebooks would be vulnerable to the same sort of attack.

The attack, which is described at http://bouk.co/blog/hacking-developers/ (cited in the guile bug report), is basically malicious a cross protocol scripting attack combined with DNS rebinding to change the attacking code's DNS record to localhost as the article puts it.

Given that Jupyter notebooks listen to a TCP port on localhost using http, it would seem that Jupyter notebooks might be even more vulnerable to the same sort of attack. I do hope I am wrong on this, though, and jupyter notebooks are immune.

The mitigation that Guile suggests, using a unix socket, would obviously not work for Jupyter notebooks. The whole point is to use them in the browser. Only thing I can think of, if it is an issue, would be to have the jupyter notebook, when it is run, generate some sort of random authentication information and print it out to the terminal and wait a bit before starting everything so that the user can copy it or make a pop-up window in a GUI environment that shows it.

issue_title: "Make a copy" menu item loses unsaved changes
issue_body:
Sometimes I find myself making changes to a notebook, then realise that I should keep my original notebook for comparison reasons.

So I use the "Make a copy" option to save a copy of the notebook with my changes (and go back to undo them from my original file). However, this make a copy of the current *notebook state on disk, neglecting any unsaved changes*.

I appreciate that this may be subjective, but this behaviour seems quite counter-intuitive to me. My expectation of this option is that it should operate more like "Save As...", so that the current contents of the notebook gets saved to a new filename.

Some translations are missing in the .pot files from the i18n folder.

Maybe the gettext syntax was not used in some places in the code.

If you find other misses, you can comment this ticket.

## File listing view

The %%bash cell does not show any output until everything is finished running. 

For example
`%%bash
echo "Hello 1"
sleep 5
echo "Hello 2"`

Will not output "Hello 1" until the whole cell is done, 5 seconds later.

My use case for this is that in my pipeline, I have some rather long process calls that ideally I would like to run in my notebook. However, not being able to inspect the output while it's running makes things difficult. Clearly for python cells this isn't a problem, can we solve it for bash cells?

I do almost everything in the notebooks, but I wish I could make quick sketches and save them too, as I do in physical notebooks.  I think jupyter notebooks would be unlike any other if they supported handwritten sketches.  

I imagine something like a "canvas" cell type, that triggers a simple javascript whiteboard in place of the cell and accepts cursor inputs for sketching.  For example, a cell that was able to produce the following:

![2016-12-20 18_18_21-untitled - paint](https://cloud.githubusercontent.com/assets/10778668/21371789/4c12aeb2-c6e1-11e6-9560-61d176a9c2d4.png)

Based on this [SO Post](https://stackoverflow.com/questions/6845682/real-time-collaborative-drawing-whiteboard-in-html5-js-and-websockets), there appear to be ways to implement simple [JS-like whiteboards](http://www.unionplatform.com/?page_id=2762).  

Before TLDR; is this a feasible request in the next versions of the notebook/jupyterlab?  Has this feature has been reviewed before?  Thank you.

issue_title: Could Sonnet support snt.SeparableConv1D in future release?
issue_body:
Dear Deepminder:

Currently Sonnet only support snt.SeparableConv2D and it is convenient to use.

Could it is possible in future release snt.SeparableConv1D be added? Since I am intensively using TensorFlow and Sonnet for processing bio-signals, so it is more attractive for Sonnet having such support at this regard.

I know snt.SeparableConv2D is a light wrapper around tf.nn.separable_conv2d, does it mean the action will only be taken after TensorFlow natively support tf.nn.separable_conv1d in order to have snt.SeparableConv1D?

Best regards

https://github.com/deepmind/sonnet/blob/7da90b8e63fb684b91a12e1e9a42999962804e3a/sonnet/python/modules/gated_rnn.py#L1129

Hi, I realized that the variable of convLSTM includes two biases if `use_bias` is set to true. And I think it is because here the convolution is performed separately on input and state. But effectively only one set of bias is necessary. Perhaps adding bias at the end instead of within the convolution as done in the tensorflow contrib version (https://github.com/tensorflow/tensorflow/pull/8891/files) would be better.

issue_title: Ignores all errors when using `noqa: X` to only ignore X
issue_body:
When using `# noqa: E501` all errors are excluded.

This is due to the code in https://github.com/PyCQA/flake8-bugbear/blob/6bf40eafe99bb569296c460b691a6c0bebd27ea5/bugbear.py#L39-L40.

`pycodestyle.noqa` searches for r'# no(?:qa|pep8)\b', which matches `# noqa: E501` also.

Without knowing the internals it seems like flake8 itself should include the errors itself - it has logic to ignore only specific errors (https://github.com/PyCQA/flake8/blob/013b925624bd5e4c026e83eb2c6e9f45cafa0dc0/src/flake8/defaults.py#L39-L51).

Example:
```python
s = 'some very, very, very, very, very, very, very, very, very, very, very, very, very, very, very long line'  # noqa: E123
```

flake8-bugbear should report error `t-noqa-colon.py:1:110: B950 line too long (109 > 80 characters)` for it, but is silent.

issue_title: Regression in 0.15 with I202 (newline in imports)
issue_body:
For a given file `repro.py`, there is a regression:

```
$ virtualenv venv
$ venv/bin/pip install flake8 flake8-import-order numpy
$ venv/bin/python repro.py
Here are my imports:
- matplotlib.pyplot: None
- numpy: <module 'numpy' from '.../venv/lib/python3.6/site-packages/numpy/__init__.py'>
- seaborn: None
- six: <module 'six' from '.../venv/lib/python3.6/site-packages/six.py'>
$
$ venv/bin/pip show flake8-import-order
Name: flake8-import-order
Version: 0.15
$ venv/bin/flake8 repro.py --import-order-style=google
repro.py:10:1: I202 Additional newline in a section of imports.
$
$ venv/bin/pip install 'flake8-import-order==0.14.3'
$ venv/bin/flake8 repro.py --import-order-style=google
$
```

----

```python
# repro.py
try:
    import matplotlib.pyplot as plt
except ImportError:
    plt = None
import numpy as np
try:
    import seaborn  # pylint: disable=unused-import
except ImportError:
    seaborn = None
import six


def main():
    """This is the entry point."""
    print('Here are my imports:')
    print('- matplotlib.pyplot: {}'.format(plt))
    print('- numpy: {}'.format(np))
    print('- seaborn: {}'.format(seaborn))
    print('- six: {}'.format(six))


if __name__ == '__main__':
    main()
```

I don't have any preference for how I do my imports, so I'm happy to change the imports to "conform" to what is expected. Are there equivalent docs? My logic was "keep them in alphabetical order and catch the potential `ImportError`-s as they come".

I have ["fixed"][1] this issue for now in a real code base just by ignoring the error.

[1]: https://github.com/dhermes/bezier/blob/4400e4f300a0b227d7610a3f20d711ea6fe0e0d9/tests/unit/test__algebraic_intersection.py#L21

Anyone want to go to Turkey?
http://www.aamas2015.com/en/DEMOS.html

The base classes for Agent and Model were put together pretty haphazardly, and are poorly documented. Fix. In particular, there are some assumptions (e.g. Agent taking a model as a constructor argument) that don't reflect most of the models we've written so far.

issue_body:
I dont have coverageerc or something like in my tornado project. 
I installed pytest-cov and typed in root folder `py.test test/`

It started tests and show me this results only.. No any other information or warning. :
============================================= test session starts =============================================
                                                                                                               
platform linux -- Python 3.6.1, pytest-3.0.7, py-1.4.34, pluggy-0.4.0

test/test_namespace.py ..                                                                                      
test/test_parameter.py ...............                                                                         
                                                                                                               
========================================== 27 passed in 0.63 seconds ==========================================

Where is the coverage information.

Hi! I have configured my project to automatically set a value of `-n` based on the number of available CPUs in the host where the tests are being run. Also, the SUT is started with a level of resources proportional to the value of `-n` so it can handle the load properly. The higher the number of resources required by the SUT the longer it takes to start (and the higher the monetary cost is, since these resources are AWS stuff).
This usually pays off really well, and thanks to xdist I've managed to reduce considerably the time it takes to run the whole test suite.

However when somebody wants to run a subset of tests, based on filters like `-k foo`, even if only 2 or 3 tests match the filter, xdist will still spawn all of the `-n` processes unnecessarily. And it will usually take longer to run with xdist than without (because of the SUT startup time)

I tried to find, with no luck, an appropriate pytest hook that kicks in after the tests have been collected but before xdist creates the worker nodes, so I can set `numprocesses = min(numprocesses, tests_collected)` and set up the SUT with the appropriate amount of resources. But it seems all the tests are collected after the worker nodes are spawned (and they are collected in every single node?).

I hope I made myself clear. Any clues on how can this be solved?

Thanks!

issue_title: Feature request: provide way to acquire latest, stable 2.1 Windows/macOS bits
issue_body:
**Is your feature request related to a problem? Please describe.**
Starting in .NET Core 2.1, the .deb/.rpm acquisition experience changed. Linux users can now reference `dotnet-sdk-2.1` and `dotnet-runtime-2.1`. This makes it easy to upgrade by just running `apt upgrade`. There is no way to get the equivalent behavior on Windows and macOS

**Describe the solution you'd like**
I'd like a way to be able to get the latest, stable `2.1` runtime and SDK. I'm not concerned about floating the patch version.

**Describe alternatives you've considered**
* Update `dotnet-install.ps1` to support a "2.1" stable channel.
* Use a stable link that I can curl/wget from such as `https://aka.ms/dotnet/2.1/dotnet-runtime-2.1-win-x64.{exe,zip}`

**Additional context**
https://github.com/travis-ci/travis-build/pull/1397#issuecomment-393988234

issue_title: Visual Studio 2015 on .NET Core: F# needs to be able to change the file order
issue_body:
File order is significant in the F# language project.
The Visual Studio .NET can do it for normal .NET projects.
But on .NET Core, project.json, the Visual Studio is not able to change the file order.

issue_title: NET CORE 2.0 - Ubuntu 14.04 - Could not load file or assembly System.Runtime.Extensions or
issue_body:
I was trying to migrate my solution of NET CORE 1.1 to NET CORE 2.0 as a linux server.
When I run dotnet xxx.DLL in Ubuntu 14.04 x64 the following problem occurs:

```
Unhandled Exception: System.IO.FileLoadException: Could not load file or assembly 'System.Runtime.Extensions, Version=4.2.0.0, Culture=neutral, PublicKeyToken=b03f5f7f11d50a3a'. The located assembly's manifest definition does not match the assembly reference. (Exception from HRESULT: 0x80131040)
   at Microsoft.AspNetCore.Hosting.WebHostBuilder..ctor()
   at Microsoft.AspNetCore.WebHost.CreateDefaultBuilder(String[] args)
   at Teste.Program.BuildWebHost(String[] args).
```
Follow some configurations of my project:

**Project.csproj**
```
<TargetFramework>netcoreapp2.0</TargetFramework>
<RuntimeIdentifiers>win10-x64;ubuntu.14.04-x64</RuntimeIdentifiers>
```

I tried to place the missing package in Nuget and the same thing did not work.

Linux server owns NET CORE 2.

Updated VS 2017 (15.3.2)

I checked if the DLL was on the server (and it was).

I tried to place the tag:
 `<AutoGenerateBindingRedirects>true</AutoGenerateBindingRedirects>`

Does anyone have a suggestion?

I made a lot of tests simple. I created a MVC NET CORE 2.0 project and I did not do anything. I compiled for Ubuntu 14.04 and when I try to run it. A similar error occurs:

`Unhandled Exception: System.IO.FileLoadException: Could not load file or assembly 'System.IO.FileSystem, Version=4.1.0.0, Culture=neutral, PublicKeyToken=b03f5f7f11d50a3a'.`

**Is there any problem as the MVC CORE 2 solution in the 14.04 x64 linux server?**

# Dotnet Core 2.0 Crash Ubuntu

My console application dotnet core 2.0 that running in ubuntu crash without explication, have several try catch but not working, this happen eventually, one day in one week.

Main application

```
         static void Main(string[] args)

issue_title: No Configurators (Authenticator and Installer) installed should suggest auth verb
issue_body:
If the user does not have any fully supported systems installed, the client should at least be informed about the 'auth' verb.

Location: after the pick_configurator call in cli.py
https://github.com/letsencrypt/letsencrypt/blob/master/letsencrypt/cli.py#L181

May be implemented with a display notification() mesage
right now website commands and --help commands don't match. please review / sync that before launch! :)

issue_title: SSL Labs Chain Issue with fullchain.pem (Beta)
issue_body:
I just signed with the Beta CA and got this from SSL Labs Server Test:

Certification Paths

We need to make a `certbot-auto` that doesn't install OS packages without prompting the user.

Question: Do we need/want to do this for the `0.6.0` release?

issue_title: letsencrypt manual authentication internal server error
issue_body:
This was following on from https://github.com/letsencrypt/letsencrypt/issues/734 

with CentOS 6.7 with Python 2.7 installed via IUS Community YUM repo for CentOS 6 https://iuscommunity.org/pages/Repos.html installed via

    yum -y install python27 python27-devel python27-pip python27-setuptools python27-tools python27-virtualenv --enablerepo=ius

running commands

     virtualenv --no-site-packages -p python2.7 venv

    ./venv/bin/letsencrypt -d le1.http2ssl.xyz auth

get

It seems that acme defines wrappers for various OpenSSL classes in order to get correct comparable behaviour. However, _pyopenssl_cert_or_req_san() uses isinstance to determine whether or not the passed object is a certificate or a request. Due to the wrapping this check gets the incorrect answer and will eventually cause OpenSSL to throw an error.

The directory `/etc/letsencrypt/live/paulschreiber.com-0001` no longer exists.

It's not that simple as #1272, because you can't use `location{}` outside `server{}`, but we could add only `include /etc/letsencrypt/nginx-http01-location.conf` to all affected `server{}` stanzas. Less messing with user's config => better experience!

issue_title: letsencrypt-auto --help should not run "apt-get install -y ..." automatically
issue_body:
I just had a machine trashed.

Running `./letsencrypt-auto --help` ran `apt-get install -y ...` automatically, which, among other things updated `libc`, trashing the entire machine to a point where re-installation was the easiest path. Sure the cause was a misconfiguration of `/etc/apt/sources` performed by us months ago, but still, `./letsencrypt-auto --help` shouldn't trash the machine because of that.

I suggest removing the `-y` switch so that the user is prompted for confirmation before apt-get does its thing, and/or a `letsencrypt-auto --yes-to-apt` switch or something. If one were to argue that the '-auto' in `letsencrypt-auto` implies automatic behaviour, then please provide a `letsencrypt-non-auto` or something *and use __that__ as the default in the documentation*.

Please don't perform an operation that can trash my entire machine by running `./letsencrypt-auto --help`. Nobody expects that. 

Or at the very least check that this doesn't install any packages you didn't anticipate. Perhaps running `apt-get install -s $packages` and testing the output. If only packages from an expected white-list show up, then run `apt-get install -y $packages` otherwise omit the -y and do `apt-get install $packages`, so the user is prompted for confirmation.

issue_title: How to use same certificate on different server (different port) but same DNS
issue_body:
I have successfully created a certificate using letsencrypt on one of my apache server.

    https://mydomain.duckdns.org

On my second server, I just forward a different port (4430) to this machine, ie, accessible by:

   https://mydomain.duckdns.org:4430

I tried the same procedure to install letsencrypt `./letsencrypt-auto --apache -d mydomain.duckdns.org` on this machine but got this error:

     The following 'urn:acme:error:unauthorized' errors were reported by
      the server:

      Domains: mydomain.duckdns.org
      Error: The client lacks sufficient authorization

Question is, how can I use the cert created by letsencrypt on my first machine on my second machine?

issue_title: letsencrypt complains if /etc/letsencrypt/renewal directory does not exist
issue_body: When I ran letsencrypt inside docker, it complained until I manually created /etc/letsencrypt/renewal.

issue_title: letsencrypt-auto generated mixed IPv6 and IPv4 vhost apache config line
issue_body:
The virtual host config for the Apache I tried to get a certificate for looks like this:

`<VirtualHost [2a01:238:429e:6b00:6188:46:8080:e1b4]:80 85.214.41.172:80>`

letsencrypt-auto generate a apache config file with the following vhost:

`<VirtualHost [2a01:443 85.214.41.172:443>`

issue_title: Update pinnings and hashes of LE packages for le-auto
issue_body: We'll need to update the pinnings and hashes of the LE packages to point to the 0.3.0 values before release if we want to publicly test le-auto. Ultimately, this should become part of the release script.

issue_title: Certificate expiration notice: send it only for the more recent certificate for a given domain

To avoid sending many emails that users might consider as "false positive" (and will end up ignoring the important ones).

issue_title: inconsistant behaviour of parse parameter dayfirst
issue_body:
I realized a change from version 2.5.1 to 2.5.2 in regards to ``parse`` and the parameter ``datefirst``. In my application I have crappy legacy dates in the format: * 2017/04/01 * 01/04/2017 Both meaning April 1st, 2017. I am using this code: 
from dateutil.parser import parse

ts = "01/04/2017"
d = parse(ts, dayfirst=True)
print("{0!r}".format(d))

ts = "2017/04/01"
d = parse(ts, dayfirst=True)
print("{0!r}".format(d))
From 2.4.2 to 2.5.1 this code will produce April 1st in both cases. Starting with 2.5.2 it will create January 4th in the second case. It looks like, the parameter ``dayfirst`` was ignored in version <=2.5.1 when parsing an American style date "2017/04/01" but is applied in newer version >=2.5.2. Is this the intended behaviour? Is there a recommendation for dealing with this change from version 2.5.1 to 2.5.2?

The following snippet takes ~ almost 0.1 seconds to run, this seems to be due to dateutil iterating over all the dates from 2000 till 2020. There may be an possibility to not loop over all the days, speeding the calculation up but only for this simple case I guess. For example starting at the between startdate, but this doesn't seem easy to implement or would have to be special cased for 'easy' recurrences.  

```
from datetime import datetime
from dateutil.rrule import DAILY, rrule
output = rrule(DAILY, dtstart=datetime(2000,1,1), until=datetime(2030,1,1)).between(datetime(2020,1,1), datetime(2020,2,1))
```

Tested on:
Dateutil 2.7.0
Python 3.6.4

issue_title: Parsing ISO date is broken when dayfirst=True (since 2.5.2 release)
issue_body:
Before 2.5.2 release, parse with dayfirst=True, parsed dates like '2015-02-06' as expected 2 – month and 06 – day. This is right, because `-` delimiter is only possible in ISO 8601. According to this list: https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Date_format_by_country, format yyyy-dd-mm is not used anywhere.

Seems, pull request https://github.com/dateutil/dateutil/pull/229 broken this behavior.

Can we please fix it, because I need to parse '2015-02-06' as 6 February, but if date was given as 06.02.15 consider first number is a day.

issue_title: Exceptions don't have enough information for users
issue_body:
As a Developer _I want_ to have `code`, `message`, `description` whenever a Watson service raises an exception _so that_ I can write code that reacts different depending on the exception.

# Context
It seems like the Python exceptions this SDK is throwing are not very useful for developers:
> **Bruce Adams:**
> I do not have a strong opinion about exception classes. I do find it irritating that `WatsonException` takes a structured error response from as service call and flattens it into a string as the exception args. When I want to make decisions based on the error code (my code needs to react differently to 429 compared to a 500), I have to pick the code out of the string.


More info:
 * https://stackoverflow.com/questions/256222/which-exception-should-i-raise-on-bad-illegal-argument-combinations-in-python
 * https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=o9pEzgHorH0
## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] Switch to use `ValueError` when there is a missing or illegal argument
- [ ] Parse the error parameters and make them available to users (`code`, `description`)

issue_title: Powerline statusline loses its colours in Vim when `:w` is used
issue_body:
`vim --version` output:
```
VIM - Vi IMproved 7.4 (2013 Aug 10, compiled Nov 24 2016 16:44:48)
Included patches: 1-1689
Extra patches: 8.0.0056
```
Using the powerline theme for vim, here is how the theme is called:
```
set rtp+=$HOME/.local/lib/python2.7/site-packages/powerline/bindings/vim/
set laststatus=2
```

Each time I use `:w` to write to a file, my powerline statusline goes from looking like this:
![image](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/29816026/28464049-ddabd66e-6e66-11e7-89af-9efa9a9713fc.png)

...to looking like this: 
![image](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/29816026/28464067-f9577080-6e66-11e7-96f3-c037ffa8232f.png)

If I use `so %` to re-source the .vimrc file in the same session, the statusline colours return as normal (until `:w` is used again)

Doing some research, if I use `:verbose set statusline?`, it outputs `statusline=%!py3eval('powerline.statusline(1)')`. Seems like somewhere the powerline theme is calling to set the `statusline` param to `1`, as opposed to the value of `2` like it should be to have it persist. 

I realise this may not be a powerline theme issue, so please close if necessary (I have also posted this in the Vim issues thread)

issue_title: minLength/maxLength being added to all types
Wrong field name in errors dict when using @validates on load_from/data_key aliased field
issue_body:
I am using marshmallow 2.14.
Given following code:
```python
from marshmallow import Schema, fields, ValidationError, validates

class Broken(Schema):
    f = fields.String(required=True, load_from='f-name')
    f2 = fields.String(required=True, load_from='f2-name')

    @validates('f')
    def validate_string(self, data):
        raise ValidationError('nope')

_, err = Broken().load({'f': 'hello', 'f2': None}) # does not throw validation error
print(err)
```
I get following output:
```
{'f': ['nope'], 'f2-name': ['Field may not be null.']}
```
But I would expect to end up with:
```
{'f-name': ['nope'], 'f2-name': ['Field may not be null.']}
```
There is seems to be no way to override this behavior.
I suppose it should use load_from field on load and dump_to on dump for errors.

issue_title: How to implement inter_byte_time like timeout?
issue_body:
I have very specific needs for serial data transmission on Linux:

1. Variable baud rates from 50 to 2M
2. Unidirectional nature with single Tx-Rx pair (I'm using Reed-Solomon error correction for packets)
3. Two very different types of packet transfer:

    1. Data the length of which changes randomly between approx. 100 and 1000 bytes. These packets need to be output at high speed. `readline()` and `read_until()` do not work because I can't trust terminator symbols transmit error free (error correction happens after receiving the packet). The method `read(n)` on the other hand requires me to setup timeout, which sucks because on average packets are 400 bytes but I need to account for rare situations where some users output up to 1000 bytes. It slows average use case down because error correction requires it to capture entire packet in single burst.

    2. Data that has no maximum limit (except maybe RAM). I can't set timeout high for this because it ruins the previous use case. I can assemble the packet from a set of `read(1000)` calls but the additional layer of logic becomes unreliable with high speeds.
             
So I need a function that can read arbitrary length sequences and where timeout happens when line is dead for a brief amount of time (not so brief system timeouts between transferred bytes, so the wide baud rate range needs to be taken into account.

I did some reading for this and discovered the `inter_byte_timeout` setting. However, an answer in [SO](https://stackoverflow.com/questions/35871822/what-is-inter-byte-timeout-interchartimeout-in-pyserial/37053542#37053542) states that the line [#321 of `serialposix.py`](https://github.com/pyserial/pyserial/blob/master/serial/serialposix.py#L321)

    vtime = int(self._inter_byte_timeout * 10)

"converts it to a vtime of zero and that's why [OPs] code fails. Set it to 0.1 or higher and it should work."

So I need to set `inter_byte_timeout` at least 0.1 after which receiver looks for 1 second break between transmissions. This is weird. Why does the transmitter need to stay quiet for 1000ms for the receiver to understand that the byte sequence is complete? Also, it doesn't even seem to work. For example

    # Transmitter
    import serial
    import time
    baudrate = 115200
    port     = serial.Serial('/dev/ttyUSB0', baudrate)
    port.write(50 * b'a')


    # Receiver:
    import serial
    baudrate = 115200
    port     = serial.Serial('/dev/ttyUSB0', baudrate, inter_byte_timeout=0.1, timeout=3)
    while True:
        read = port.read(100)
        print(len(read))

timeouts 3, not 1 second after receiving the 50 bytes. It does however return immediately if transmitter is run twice (it receives the 100 bytes `read()` waits for).

So I started wondering if there's a reason tighter byte-sequence timeout can't be made. If I couldn't do it, I'd run in to the reason. However, following proof of concept code seems to work with any transmission length from 1 to 1M in  baud rates from 50 to 2M.


    # Transmitter.py
    # Python 3.3 or newer only
    import os
    import random
    import serial
    import time

    baudrate       = 2000000
    msg_length     = 100

    bauds_per_byte = 10
    bytes_per_s    = baudrate / bauds_per_byte
    byte_travel_t  = 1 / bytes_per_s
    delay          = max(4 * byte_travel_t, 0.02)
    port           = serial.Serial('/dev/ttyUSB0', baudrate)

    print('Delay: {}ms'.format(delay*1000))

    while True:
        try:
            port.write(msg_length * b'a')
            port.flush()
            time.sleep(delay)
        except KeyboardInterrupt:
            exit()

---

    # Receiver.py
    # Python 3.3 or newer only
    import serial
    import os
    import time

    baudrate       = 2000000
    msg_length     = 100
    
    bauds_per_byte = 10
    bytes_per_s    = baudrate / bauds_per_byte
    byte_travel_t  = 1 / bytes_per_s

    # If next byte doesn't arrive by the time it takes to arrive, timeout.
    # When speed grows above 1ms timeout resolution, do not set lower value.
    timeout        = max(2 * byte_travel_t, 0.01)
    port           = serial.Serial('/dev/ttyUSB1', baudrate)
    
    print('Timeout:  {}ms'.format(timeout*1000))

    while True:
        try:
            read_buffer = bytearray()
            while True:
                read = os.read(port.fd, 1000)
                if read:
                    start_time = time.monotonic()
                    read_buffer.extend(read)
                else:
                    if read_buffer:
                        delta = time.monotonic() - start_time
                        if delta > timeout:
                            print('Delta: {}'.format(delta))
                            print(read_buffer == msg_length * b'a')
                            break
                    else:
                        time.sleep(0.001)
        except KeyboardInterrupt:
            exit()

Accessing the low level `os.read` for serial object's file descriptor isn't exactly ideal, plus this implementation loses pyserial's error handling etc. So my question is, can I somehow implement this kind of logic with existing code and if not, can this type of `read()` be added to the library?

Thank You.

issue_title: Make source install easier for Python 3.6
issue_body:
When building from source on Python 3.6, there are three packages where we use a version that does not have a py36 wheel, but could really need one: numpy, cython, scikit-learn

Packages like numpy and scikit-learn takes a really long time to build. Cython is probably quicker, but actually fails for me since its `setup.py` tries to import `numpy`, which has not been installed yet. I think there are two easy solutions and one a bit ugly, but I'll throw it in anyway:

## 1 
For loop over the requirements, to ensure numpy is properly installed first before proceeding with cython. Like this:

```diff
diff --git a/scripts/install_python_toolchain.sh b/scripts/install_python_toolchain.sh
index 9f7cf4efc..5be6c3034 100755
--- a/scripts/install_python_toolchain.sh
+++ b/scripts/install_python_toolchain.sh
@@ -52,7 +52,9 @@ function download_file {
 } # end of download file
 
 $python_scripts/$PIP install --upgrade "pip>=8.1"
-$python_scripts/$PIP install -r scripts/requirements.txt
+for package in $(cat scripts/requirements.txt); do
+  $python_scripts/$PIP install $package
+done
 
 mkdir -p deps/local/lib
 mkdir -p deps/local/include
```
This should fix the build (both now, and possibly later when py37 ruins everything again), but it will still be slow.

## 2
Second solution is to bump the versions so that they support py36 wheels. These are actually modest bumps that I don't think will affect testing much. Especially numpy, which is a patch bump:

- numpy: 1.11.1 -> 1.11.3
- cython 0.24 -> 0.25.2
- scikit-learn 0.17.1 -> 0.18.1

## 3 (the ugly one)
It's also easy to do versions conditioned on the Python. I don't really suggest this as a solution, but it could be used in corner cases where we really want to test with a version in one python that is tricky to install on another python. This is actually what I've been doing locally, just to conform to the defaults in my py2 environment, while still making my py3 install a breeze:

```diff
diff --git a/scripts/requirements.txt b/scripts/requirements.txt
index b15b9a639..b6211e8c6 100644
--- a/scripts/requirements.txt
+++ b/scripts/requirements.txt
@@ -1,7 +1,9 @@
 coremltools==2.0b1
 scipy==0.19.1
-numpy==1.11.1
-cython==0.24
+numpy==1.11.1; python_version < '3.6'
+numpy==1.11.3; python_version >= '3.6'
+cython==0.24; python_version < '3.6'
+cython==0.25.2; python_version >= '3.6'
 Jinja2==2.8
 argparse==1.2.1
 decorator==4.1.2
@@ -12,7 +14,8 @@ pillow==5.0.0
 prettytable==0.7.2
 pytz==2016.3
 requests>=2.9.1
-scikit-learn==0.17.1
+scikit-learn==0.17.1; python_version < '3.6'
+scikit-learn==0.18.1; python_version >= '3.6'
 six==1.10.0
 statsmodels==0.8.0
 wheel==0.29.0
```
---
I suggest using solution 1 *and* 2, and 3 only if there is strong reason to keep one of the package versions exactly as is.

issue_title: Conventions for keeping track of creation- and modification-dates
issue_body:
An unfortunate aspect of the HDF5 format is that opening a file makes the operating system treat it as modified, even if nothing changed and even if it is opened in read-only mode. On Windows this results in a changed modification date, and on Linux and OSX there is no distinction between modification or creation dates to begin with.

It would be useful to be able to keep track of *real* changes to loom files. This could be used to trigger automatic updates in various work-flows, for example. Having different levels of granularity about what was changed would also be useful here. It would also be useful if there was a convention for this, so that modification-detecting scripts from different groups would work with each other's files without much trouble. If the `loompy` library would inherently keep track of and update these modification tracking attributes, things would be even easier, since people would not have to think about it.

To give a concrete example: to serve data from a loom file to a website, a `loom-viewer` server must extract the data from the loom file and convert it to JSON. This is a relatively slow process, and on top of that h5py does not like it when an HDF5 file is opened by multiple processes (even in read-only mode). So to mitigate this issue, whenever JSON data is generated it is also cached as a zipped static file. The next time someone requests that data, the static file is served instead of repeating the whole process. 

The problems start if the data in the loom file is modified (for example, when a column attribute is added to loom file). At this point, existing JSON files that are outdated have to be replaced. It is currently not possible to detect when to do this automatically - it needs to be done manually by whomever is modifying the loom file.

One way around this would be to have a global attribute, or multiple attributes, that are used to keep track of real file changes. Being able to distinguish different kinds of modifications would be nice too. For the `loom-viewer` the following level of precision is enough:

- file metadata, 
- attributes (global, row and column)
- the data matrix as a whole

But perhaps other people have a use for more fine-grained checks (detecting which rows were changed, for example).

I would like to hear the thoughts of others on this, and come up with a shared proposal for how to handle this.

Is the `row_graphs` group mandatory or optional?

The specification says:

> There MUST be a group `/row_graphs`

But it doesn't appear in the example at http://linnarssonlab.org/loompy/format/index.html#example

After a little digging, I discovered the following if/set line appears to be reliably causing the problem:

        if varName not in self.localVars:

                'var': z3.Int(varName),

        'x': {
            'var': x, # This is the actual z3.Int/z3.Array, etc object
            'expr': [
                'localVars['x']['var'] > 1',    # Actual expressions to go into solver
                'localVars['x']['var'] * 5 > 7'
            ]
        }
    }
```

As I mentioned, the code works while running, but it appears that the exit procedure might be clearing up the links to the z3 variables before the variables are free'd themselves? In any case, not sure what to do about this.

Mate Schwerhoff previously reported a bug on codeplex (see [here](http://z3.codeplex.com/workitem/190)), migrating this to the new issue tracker. In his own words:

Given the program below, Z3 4.3.2 and 4.4.0 55ca6ce44b2f (both Win x64) immediately report unknown, despite the fact that the given terms and patterns should allow it to instantiate each forall once, thereby showing that the goal is unsat.

Am I missing something here that would explain why Z3 doesn't perform the instantiation, or is this a bug in the triggering code?

issue_title: "Overflow encountered when expanding vector" during solving
issue_body:
I have several queries where Z3 aborts with `Overflow encountered when expanding vector`. In some cases this error comes after only a few seconds for not-too-big formulas. I am currently using Z3 4.5.0-124-gc1480b4, but I can reproduce this with the latest git revision.

So I am wondering whether
- this might be a bug that leads to unexpectedly high memory usage?
- something could be optimized to solve such formulas with less memory? The query actually does not seem to be that difficult: If I serialize the solver and give the resulting SMTLIB2 formula to Z3, it can be solved instantly.
- Z3 could be changed to allow the use of larger vectors? I have a 64bit system and would be fine with Z3 taking a few more gigabytes for solving the formula (I am currently using a memory limit of 15 GB, and Z3 just uses 4 and then gives up).

The queries where I see this problem all use linear arithmetic over Ints and UFs, some of them also use arrays and/or quantifiers. Interestingly, I have not encountered this problem for bitvector queries so far.
I cannot remember seeing this problem earlier, it looks like it is related to a change in Z3 during the last months?

Here is an [archive](http://students.fim.uni-passau.de/~wendler/z3-overflow-encountered.tar.bz2) with a replay log and the same query as serialized solver output for one of my problems. If you need more information, just tell me.

This is on the unstable branch.

The following benchmark:

```
(set-option :produce-models true)
(set-logic QF_FP)
(declare-fun s0 () (_ FloatingPoint 11 53))
(assert
   (let ((s1 (fp.isSubnormal s0)))
   s1))
(check-sat)
(get-value (s0))
```

Results in:

```
sat
((s0 (_ +zero 11 53)))
```

which suggests `+0` is a sub-normal number, which isn't true.

Z3 produces unknown on the following problem:

(declare-const I Int)

The proper way to build a distributable tarball is to do something like

```
$ python scripts/mk_make.py <flags to enable bindings>
$ cd build
$ make
$ mkdir staged
$ make DESTDIR=`readlink -f staged` install
$ tar -C staged -czvf z3.tar.gz .
```

The newly added ``DESTDIR`` support makes a lot of the existing python code ``mk_unix_dist()`` a bit pointless because now creating a distributable is basically the same as doing a staged install but then tarballing the result.

issue_title: z3.parse_smt2_string() in python API fails when string contains UTF-8 characters
issue_body:
From SMT-LIBv2.6 UTF-8 can be used in `.smt2` files. The python API `z3.parse_smt2_string()` fails to work on these files because it assumes the string it is provided with must be ascii.

Here's how to reproduce

Here's the contents of `test.py`

```python
#!/usr/bin/env python
import io
import logging
import sys
import z3

_logger = logging.getLogger(__name__)

def parse(query_file):
    assert isinstance(query_file, io.TextIOWrapper)
    # Load query_file as string
    query_str = ""
    for l in query_file.readlines():
        query_str += str(l)
    _logger.debug('query:\n{}'.format(query_str))
    try:
        constraint = z3.parse_smt2_string(query_str)
    except z3.z3types.Z3Exception as e:
        msg = 'Parsing failed: {}'.format(e)
        return (None, msg)
    return (constraint, None)

def main(args):
    logging.basicConfig(level=logging.DEBUG)
    file_name = args[0]
    with open(file_name, 'r') as f:
        contraint, msg = parse(f)
    return 0
        

if __name__ == '__main__':
    sys.exit(main(sys.argv[1:]))
```

I hacked around the problem in the `_to_ascii` function by changing it to

```python
def _to_ascii(s):
  if isinstance(s, str):
    return s.encode(encoding='utf-8')
  else:
    return s

This is only a hack though. Instead the Python API implementation needs to be changed to not assume that the string containing SMT-LIBv2 is ASCII.

So now that we're on the topic of requirements (#15, #19) -- I'm not sure using `future` in a library like this (esp. `future.standard_library.install_aliases()`) is a good idea.

I mean, just importing `upcloud_api` as it is [will install magical `sys.meta_path` hooks](https://github.com/PythonCharmers/python-future/blob/master/src/future/standard_library/__init__.py#L521-L538).

Maybe just use `six` (as it's a dep too anyway)?

issue_title: Unify keyword argument names for convolution and pooling
issue_body:
The keyword argument names for 1D and {2,3}D convolution and pooling are currently not the same.

* 1D-convolution has `filter_length` and `stride`
* {2,3}D-convolution has `filter_size` and `strides`
* {1,2,3}D-pooling has `ds` and `st` in #199

I'd suggest to use `filter_size` and `stride` for all convolutions, and `pool_size` and `stride` for all poolings.
For a really uniform interface, it would be nice if all of `filter_size`, `pool_size` and `stride` either accepted a single integer or a tuple of integers matching the dimensionality.

Arguments for or against it?
Is there a way to make use of [pytest's pretty assertion rewriting](https://docs.pytest.org/en/latest/example/reportingdemo.html#tbreportdemo) with nbval?

issue_title: `CreateFailed` should wrap the underlying error if any
issue_body:
Hi Will, 
while I was working on `fs.sshfs`, I noticed that since `fs.open_fs` is expected to always raise `CreateFailed` when something goes wrong, it's not really easy to report what really went wrong.

I think that the `CreateFailed` exception should probably expose a `exc` attribute, like `OperationFailed` does. This way, it would be easier to manage the failed creation, depending on whether it comes from a `PermissionError`, a `TimeoutError`, a `ResourceNotFound` when `create=False`, etc. 

What do you think about this ?

It behaves as it is documented

Since FS objects have no concept of a current directory, this simply adds a leading / character if the path doesn’t already have one.

But

```
>>> fs.path.abspath(u'webdav://some.dav.de')
u'/webdav://some.dav.de'
```
However if it  finds a fs protocoll it should not do that.

issue_body:
Hello, 

I am currently experimenting with symbolic execution on AArch64 and have a problem that a path errors with the message `ValueError("invalid literal for int() with base 16: ''")`. 

The last Vex block before the path errors looks like this:


```
   00 | ------ IMark(0x100007cf4, 4, 0) ------
   01 | t0 = GET:I64(xsp)
   02 | t1 = Add64(t0,0xffffffffffffffe0)

   25 | ------ IMark(0x100007d08, 4, 0) ------
   26 | t15 = LDle:I64(0x0000000100008000)
```


While debugging I found that the problem is in Vex Statement 26. I tracked down the error to the file claripy/ast/bv.py. If the variable value is an empty string, the marked line raises a ValueError: 

```

def BVV(value, size=None, **kwargs):
    '''
    Creates a bit-vector value (i.e., a concrete value).

    :param value:   The value.
    :param size:    The size (in bits) of the bit-vector.

    :returns:       A BV object representing this value.
    '''

    if type(value) is str:
        if size is None:
            size = 8*len(value)
            value = int(value.encode('hex'), 16)   # THE VALUE ERROR HAPPENS HERE
        elif size == len(value)*8:
            value = int(value.encode('hex'), 16)
        else:
            raise ClaripyValueError('string/size mismatch for BVV creation')
    elif size is None:
        raise ClaripyValueError('BVV() takes either an integer value and a size or a string of bytes')

```

So, the empty string has to be handled correctly. This is not the case at the moment. I could now just also raise a ClaripyValueError but I am not sure if this is the correct way to handle it or if there is another problem. 

What do you guys think?

Regards

issue_title: Proposal for improving handling of broken (sub)jobs
issue_body:
Hi all,

There are some users again reporting that the monitoring isn't picking up DIRAC jobs with subjobs in the `submitting` state.

I can't think of any meaningful way that _any_ of the current backends can deal with monitoring a job in the `submitting` state. I could be wrong, but to me if the job is in this state in the XML it indicates that something failed.
If a (sub)job has been loaded from disk and is in this state then the (sub)job is, I suspect by definition in a bad state. To get here something has crashed/failed or ganga was force exited or killed during the job submission so some or all of the monitoring information has probably been lost.


Can we consider force failing a job automatically if it's loaded from disk in a `submittting` state?


These (sub)jobs seem to be confusing the monitoring as some people are still seeing ganga struggling to update jobs with a subjob in the `submitting` state.

I think a short PR could accomplish this from the XML layer but what are people's opinions on this?

Rob

issue_title: How many values should we take as a threshold to start coercing into category?
issue_body:
10? 20? 50? (number of states)
It seems too error prone for now.

Maybe we shouldn't coerce into categorical at all?

According to [pandas documentation](http://pandas.pydata.org/pandas-docs/stable/categorical.html):

```
The categorical data type is useful in the following cases:

* A string variable consisting of only a few different values. Converting such a string variable to a categorical variable will save some memory, see here. 
* The lexical order of a variable is not the same as the logical order (“one”, “two”, “three”). By converting to a categorical and specifying an order on the categories, sorting and min/max will use the logical order instead of the lexical order, see here. 
* As a signal to other python libraries that this column should be treated as a categorical variable (e.g. to use suitable statistical methods or plot types). 
```

Some thoughts about the reasons:
1. Saving memory doesn't seem to be much of a concern for this library.
2. The lexical order will help the min/max functions be correct when we group by columns when plotting.
3. The category will be important when select an X and Y axis automatically if we treat `categorical` as `O` in vegalite.
4. It also might be important if plotly (or any other library we use in the future) respects the order of categorical values.

As a workaround, the user could choose to execute 
```
%%sql -o df
SELECT * FROM table
```
and then execute something like
```
%%local
df[column_name] = df[column_name].astype('category')
df
```
herself if desired.

(cc @ellisonbg)

issue_title: Setting up a practical use case starting from this repository
issue_body:
I am very excited that I finally managed to get a Jupyter notebook, using external Spark resources, to run from a Docker container thanks to the work done in this repository. However, now I am struggling to translate this into a practical use case.

I am currently running into a few snags (I have only basic experience using Spark or Docker), and would like to seek help on this list. I would like to handle this step-by-step and not expose all the issues I am facing straight away, to keep this thread clear. (I am not claiming any of these issues have to do with the code base, they are simply due to my lack of expertise.)

My eventual goal is to have a containerized application (either running a Jupyter notebook, or even better, standalone python scripts) that can be deployed on any Spark cluster (e.g. on Azure or Amazon). Furthermore, for testing the way this repository is set up seems ideal. I do not want to setup Azure resources during a testing and debugging phase.

My starting point is a fresh clone of this repository, on which I ran the docker commands to build and run the Spark and Jupyter containers, i.e.
```
git clone https://github.com/jupyter-incubator/sparkmagic.git
docker-compose build
docker-compose up
```
I am now able to run a Jupyter notebook on localhost:8888, and the pyspark3 kernel does its job (I prefer to use Python3).

### Issue 1

Currently, the numpy package is not recognised. Adding the line `RUN pip install numpy` to the file `Dockerfile.jupyter` and rebuilding, does not install the numpy package in the same environment as used by the pyspark3 kernel. 

How can I install package X in my jupyter container?

An interesting observation from running the Jupyter pyspark3 kernel is that I am accessing different environments when using `%%bash` or the os library:
```
%%bash
ls /home
--------
jovyan
```

```
os.listdir("/home")
--------
[]
```

### Issue 2

This is very much related to issue 1. My reason for wanting a Jupyter notebook to run in a container without the need to include an entire Spark installation is to keep the container small and allow for the possibility to run it locally as easily as having it use a remote cluster. It would also be very convenient for workshops and/or demos. 

How can I set up my own Jupyter docker container, containing all the python packages I need so that it can be easily deployed anywhere, using any Spark resources?

The WebUI uses a Python logger to make its log. furthermore, the uWSGI also makes its own log.

As a result,we may have several log files:

 - [ ] webui-access.log, from uWSGI, that will contain the uWSGI HTTP requests
 - [ ] webui-error.log, from uWSGI, that will contain the WebUI console log
 - [ ] info.log, from WebUI, that will contain the INFO level log
 - [ ] warning.log, from WebUI, that will contain the WARNING level log
 - [ ] error.log, from WebUI, that will contain the ERROR level log

After some experiments, the 3 last configured files are not really interesting. Only both first files are pertinent. As such, I will make a default configuration with only the uWSGI log files activated.

Changing the application log level is done thanks to the `logging.json` file or an environment variable to set the application DEBUG mode.

issue_title: Check why not all cells are plotted in M1 example
Add new test for "Uploading the same Content Unit twice"
issue_body:
As per this [issue](https://pulp.plan.io/issues/1406), there is a new regression in Pulp 2.8 Beta when one tries to upload the same content (ie. rpms, puppet modules, etc) to an existing repository. We need a new automated test that does the following:

* Create a new feed-less repository
* Manually import a valid content type (i.e. RPM, etc)
  * Assert that the content type was successfully uploaded into the repository
* Manually try to import the same exact content type to the same repository
  * Assert that a PulpCodedException is raised

We should make sure that all valid content types (rpms, puppet modules, docker images, etc) are used for this test to make sure that we get the same, expected behavior across the board.

issue_title: Test the RPM counters in the sync report
issue_body:
This test case is for pulp_rpm 2.11.1+, related to this redmine issue: https://pulp.plan.io/issues/2457

Sync report looks similar to this:
```
"result": {
    ...
    "added_count": 40, 
    "result": "success", 
    "updated_count": 0, 
    "details": {
      "content": {
        "size_total": 59620, 
        "items_left": 0, 
        "items_total": 32, 
        "state": "FINISHED", 
        "size_left": 0, 
        "details": {
          "rpm_total": 32, 
          "rpm_done": 32, 
          "drpm_total": 0, 
          "drpm_done": 0
        }, 
   ...
  }
```
`rpm_*` and `items_*` counters are used only when you actually need to download something.
`added_count` - for any units which are added.
`updated_count` is 0 for the first sync but it is likely to be non-zero for all subsequent syncs and it equals to a number of all the units in the repo except non-updated RPMs (for now all the units for rpm plugin except RPM/SRPM/DRPM are updated in db during each sync no matter what).

Suggested test case:
1. Sync  the [fixture/rpm-unsigned repo ](https://repos.fedorapeople.org/pulp/pulp/fixtures/rpm-unsigned/).
2. Assert that:
- added_count == 40
- items_total == rpm_total == rpm_done == 32
- updated_count == 0
3. Remove one RPM unit.
4. Clean orphans.
5. Remove another RPM unit.
6. Re-sync.
7. Assert that:
- added_count == 2
- items_total == rpm_total == rpm_done == 1
- updated_count == 8
